We Analyzed 485 SOC 2 Reports.
Here's What Investors Need to Know.

We analyzed the technical infrastructure behind $291M+ in tracked venture funding — from a16z-backed unicorns to bootstrapped startups. Companies backed by Andreessen Horowitz, Benchmark, Kleiner Perkins, Lightspeed, Union Square Ventures, and Y Combinator. The finding that surprised us most: the amount of money raised has zero correlation with tech maturity.

A quantitative deep-dive into the tech maturity of 485 early-stage AI and SaaS companies. We extracted infrastructure data, scored security posture, mapped vendor ecosystems, and built the first open benchmark for tech due diligence at scale.

The Problem with Tech Due Diligence

Every VC and PE firm conducts tech due diligence before investing. But the process is manual, inconsistent, and expensive. A senior engineer spends 2-3 days reading a SOC 2 report, cross-referencing architecture claims with reality, and producing a qualitative assessment that's hard to compare across portfolio companies.

We took a different approach. We built an automated pipeline that reads the full PDF — every page, every diagram, every control description — and extracts structured data. Then we scored each company across 7 dimensions using a consistent rubric. The result: the first quantitative benchmark for tech due diligence across 485 companies.

This isn't a sample. It's 485 real compliance reports from companies that have completed SOC 2 audits. The majority are early-stage AI and SaaS startups — exactly the companies that VCs and growth equity firms evaluate every day.

JP
Jacek Podoba
CEO, Altimi.com
MH
Maksym Huczyński
CTO, WhiteContext.com
March 2026

The problem compounds at portfolio scale. A growth equity firm with 20 portfolio companies has 20 SOC 2 reports sitting in a shared drive, each reviewed by a different person at a different time with no shared framework. There's no way to answer basic portfolio questions: "What percentage of our companies have multi-region failover?" or "Which companies are single-cloud dependent?" or "How many are still running template-placeholder compliance?"

This report changes that. What follows is not a qualitative opinion — it's a data-driven analysis of what 485 compliance reports reveal about startup infrastructure, security posture, vendor dependencies, and operational maturity. Every chart is reproducible. Every data point is extracted from a real PDF.

Methodology

Each PDF was converted to page images and processed through a vision AI model (Moonshot kimi-k2.5, 256K context). The model extracted company metadata, infrastructure details, security controls, vendor lists, and architecture diagram components. Each company was scored 1-10 across 7 dimensions: Infrastructure, Application Architecture, Data Layer, Security, DevOps, BCDR, and Vendor Diversity. All data is stored in a Cloudflare D1 database and served via a searchable API.

How We Built the Pipeline

The technical pipeline behind this analysis processes each PDF through four stages. First, every page of every PDF is converted to a high-resolution image (150 DPI JPEG) and uploaded to cloud object storage. Second, a vision AI model (Moonshot kimi-k2.5, with a 256K token context window) analyzes all pages simultaneously in a single inference call. The model extracts structured JSON matching a 22-field schema covering infrastructure, security controls, vendor dependencies, compliance details, and architecture patterns.

Third, a separate AI call scores the extracted data across seven dimensions using a consistent rubric. Infrastructure is scored based on cloud redundancy, IaC adoption, and CDN/WAF presence. Security is scored on control depth: MFA, IDS/IPS, pen testing frequency, and monitoring tooling. DevOps maturity considers CI/CD specificity, branch protection, and deployment practices. BCDR evaluates backup frequency, DR testing evidence, and failover capability. Vendor Diversity measures the breadth and transparency of third-party dependency disclosure.

Fourth, the scored data is stored in a Cloudflare D1 database and made available through a searchable API. The entire pipeline — from PDF upload to searchable database entry — runs autonomously on Cloudflare's edge network using Workers, Workflows, R2 storage, and D1 SQL. Processing 485 reports at 80 pages each (approximately 38,000 pages total) completed in under two hours of wall-clock time.

Limitations: Vision AI extraction is imperfect. Network diagrams with small text or complex overlapping elements may lose detail. Template-heavy reports with identical boilerplate across companies can inflate certain feature detection rates (e.g., a template that mentions "IDS/IPS" whether or not the company actually deploys it). Where possible, we cross-referenced architecture diagram content against system description text to validate claims. Of 485 companies, 121 received complete dimension scoring; the remaining 364 have binary feature extraction (presence/absence) but not weighted scores.

The Score Landscape

Of 121 companies with complete dimension scores, the average tech DD score is 5.6/10. The distribution clusters tightly around 5-6 — most companies achieve a similar baseline from template-driven compliance. Only 19 companies (16%) score 7 or above.

The tight clustering tells an important story: SOC 2 compliance creates a floor, not a ceiling. Nearly every company has MFA, RBAC, and a VPC — the audit template ensures it. What separates a 5 from a 7 isn't the presence of controls, but the depth and specificity of implementation.

Consider what a score of 5 means in practice: the company has MFA enabled, uses a VPC, has firewalls and IDS/IPS, and performs basic vulnerability scanning. These are the controls that the compliance template prescribes — every auditor includes them, and every company implements them (or at least claims to). A score of 7, by contrast, means the company has gone beyond the template: they've deployed WAF, tested their DR plan, established quarterly access reviews, and — critically — named their actual tech stack rather than using generic descriptions.

The difference between a 5 and a 7 is not about having more controls on paper. It's about having an engineering team that makes deliberate infrastructure decisions and documents them transparently. That's a fundamentally different signal than "we passed the audit."

Dimension Breakdown

Security is the strongest dimension at 6.9/10 — baseline security controls are effectively universal. Vendor Diversity is the weakest at 4.3/10 — most companies depend on a single cloud provider and disclose few third-party dependencies.

Key insight: Infrastructure score has the highest correlation with overall score. Companies that invest in their infrastructure — multi-AZ, IaC, proper VPCs — tend to score higher across all dimensions. It's the foundation that lifts everything else.

The Cloud Infrastructure Landscape

AWS dominates at 59% of the portfolio. This creates a systemic risk that few portfolio managers account for: a major AWS regional outage would simultaneously impact over half the companies in a typical fund.

An emerging pattern we call "PaaS-first" architecture: a meaningful minority of companies build on Supabase, Vercel, or Render as their primary infrastructure. These platforms offer faster time-to-market but trade away control. Critically, when a company uses Supabase as their primary provider with a carve-out method, the most important infrastructure controls — physical security, network backbone, hypervisor isolation — are explicitly outside the audit scope.

This matters for investors because the carve-out method is the norm, not the exception. When a SOC 2 report says "AWS is the subservice organization using the carve-out method," it means all physical security, network backbone, hypervisor isolation, and data center environmental controls are explicitly excluded from the audit scope. The auditor is saying: "We didn't test any of that — go read AWS's own SOC 2 report." For a company where 100% of the infrastructure runs on a single cloud provider, this means the most critical controls are the ones nobody audited.

Multi-cloud strategies remain rare — fewer than 10% of companies use more than one cloud provider for production workloads. The practical implication: cloud provider selection is a one-way door for most early-stage companies, and switching costs increase with time. Investors should evaluate cloud provider choice not just for today's architecture but for the company's ability to scale, negotiate pricing, and maintain operational independence.

Portfolio risk: With 59% on AWS and only 28% deploying multi-region, a single AWS region failure would affect roughly 230 companies with no failover capability.

The Security Maturity Ladder

Security maturity across the portfolio follows a clear three-tier pattern. Understanding these tiers is essential for benchmarking a company during diligence.

TIER 1 — TABLE STAKES
80%+

MFA, RBAC, VPC, Multi-AZ, Firewalls, IDS/IPS. These are universal — their presence tells you nothing about quality.

TIER 2 — DIFFERENTIATORS
40-80%

Branch protection, pen testing, daily backups, WAF. These separate the middle from the top.

TIER 3 — ADVANCED
<40%

Multi-region, cyber insurance, quarterly reviews. Rare, expensive, and predictive of the highest scores.

The question for investors isn't "does this company have MFA?" — they all do. The question is: "Which Tier 2 and Tier 3 features have they adopted?" Companies with WAF + multi-region + quarterly reviews are in the top 5% of the portfolio.

Why does this three-tier structure exist? It's a direct consequence of how SOC 2 compliance works. The audit template — provided by firms like Accorp Partners, who audited the vast majority of companies in this dataset — prescribes Tier 1 controls as the default. Every company gets MFA, RBAC, and a VPC because the template says they should. Tier 2 controls require actual engineering decisions beyond the template: deploying WAF means choosing a provider, configuring rules, and monitoring alerts. Conducting a penetration test means hiring a third party and fixing what they find.

Tier 3 controls are rare because they require sustained organizational commitment, not just a one-time setup. Quarterly access reviews mean someone audits every user account four times a year. Multi-region means maintaining infrastructure in two or more geographic locations with active replication. Cyber insurance means an underwriter evaluated your risk profile and agreed to cover it. These aren't checkboxes — they're ongoing operational disciplines.

Vendor Transparency = Engineering Culture

Perhaps our most surprising finding: the number of vendors a company names in their SOC 2 report strongly correlates with their overall tech DD score.

Companies that name 0-1 vendors average 5.1/10. Companies that name 5+ vendors average 6.1/10. That's nearly a full point — highly significant in a distribution this tight.

This isn't because more vendors make you more secure. It's because transparency about dependencies is a proxy for engineering culture maturity. Teams that know their full vendor stack well enough to document it in an audit tend to have more deliberate architecture decisions, better monitoring, and stronger operational practices.

We see this play out in specific patterns. Companies that name their database engine (PostgreSQL, not just "database"), their CI/CD tool (GitHub Actions, not just "source code management tool"), and their monitoring stack (Datadog + Sentry, not just "monitoring tool") are telling you something about their engineering culture. They know what they're running and they're willing to put it in an audited document. Companies that use generic descriptions are either hiding complexity they don't understand or haven't made deliberate technology choices.

The most common vendors across the portfolio reveal the emerging startup infrastructure stack: AWS for compute, Supabase or Neon for databases, Clerk or STYTCH for authentication, Vercel for frontend hosting, OpenAI for LLM capabilities, Twilio for communications, and Sentry for error monitoring. Companies that assemble this full stack — and name each piece — tend to be the ones with genuine engineering leadership rather than outsourced compliance.

Due diligence signal: If a SOC 2 report names only "AWS" and nothing else, that's a transparency red flag. Ask the founder: "What's your monitoring stack? What databases do you use? What's your CI/CD pipeline?" If they can't answer specifically, the compliance report isn't the only thing that's generic.

The BCDR Gap: Policy vs Practice

100% of companies have a BCDR policy. That's the good news. The bad news: the drop-off from policy to practice is dramatic.

Only 51% test their disaster recovery plans annually. Only 28% have multi-region failover. Only 22% carry cybersecurity insurance.

Most companies in this portfolio have a disaster recovery plan they've never tested, for a disaster they can't recover from quickly, with no insurance to cover the loss. That's not resilience — it's a checkbox.

Ask for proof: During diligence, request the last DR test results — the date, the scenario, the measured recovery time, and what was fixed afterward. A company that can produce this has genuine operational maturity. One that can't has a policy document, not a capability.

Red Flags: What to Watch For

We identified and categorized thousands of red flags across the portfolio. The top flags are systemic — they appear in the majority of reports because they reflect the compliance template's defaults, not company-specific failures.

No RTO/RPO disclosed — The most common flag. Despite having BCDR policies and annual testing, almost no company commits to specific recovery time objectives.

Short audit period (3 months) — Nearly universal. The minimum viable period for a Type 2 report. Suggests a first-time SOC 2 with limited operational history.

Privacy/Processing Integrity excluded — Particularly concerning for AI companies handling user data. Many exclude these trust criteria as "not relevant" — a claim that deserves scrutiny.

Template artifacts — Published reports containing placeholder text ("Your Name Here"), sample diagrams, or highlighted instructions that were never removed. This doesn't mean controls are absent — but it undermines confidence in audit rigor.

The AI Stack Inside the Portfolio

A significant majority of these companies are AI-first. Their compliance reports reveal the emerging AI infrastructure stack — what they use, who they depend on, and where the concentration risks lie.

OpenAI dominates as the LLM provider of choice, with Anthropic and Google Gemini gaining ground. A small number of companies run self-hosted models (Llama via FIXIE, vLLM on Modal) — these tend to have more sophisticated infrastructure and carry higher IP value.

The emerging AI stack: LLM API (OpenAI/Anthropic) + Voice (ElevenLabs + Twilio) + Vector DB (Pinecone/Weaviate) + Auth (Clerk/STYTCH) + Hosting (Vercel/AWS) + Monitoring (Sentry/Datadog). Companies that name this full stack score materially higher on tech DD.

Follow the Money: Funding vs Tech Maturity

We tracked funding data for the top-scoring companies in the portfolio. The results upend a common assumption in venture capital: that well-funded companies have better technology.

$291M+
Total Funding Tracked
59
Unique Investors
10/27
YC-Backed Companies

11x AI — the highest-funded company at $75M+ with a $350M valuation, backed by Andreessen Horowitz and Benchmark — scores 6/10 on tech DD. Meanwhile, Scribeberry — a bootstrapped Canadian healthtech with no disclosed venture funding — achieves the highest score in the entire portfolio at 8/10.

This isn't an anomaly — it's a pattern. Across 27 researched companies spanning from pre-seed to Series B, we found zero meaningful correlation between funding level and tech DD score. Companies that raised $500K have architectures comparable to those that raised $50M.

CompanyFundingScoreKey Investors
11x AI$75M+6/10a16z, Benchmark, Lux Capital
Tutor Intelligence$42M6/10Union Square Ventures, Neo
Judgment Labs$30M+7/10Lightspeed, SV Angel
Ziina$30M+7/10Altos Ventures, YC, FJ Labs
Parahelp$21.2M7/10Alt Capital, YC, Perplexity Fund
Cluely$20.3M6/10a16z ($120M valuation)
Context$15.75M6/10Lux, General Catalyst, Qualcomm
Morph~$19M7/10YC, Mistral AI
ScribeberryBootstrapped8/10No venture funding disclosed

The investor pattern: Y Combinator is the single most common investor across this cohort (10 of 27 companies). Andreessen Horowitz backs three companies with combined valuations exceeding $470M. Kleiner Perkins and First Round Capital appear in the seed rounds of companies that score 6-7. But investor pedigree, like funding amount, does not predict tech DD score — it predicts market access, brand credibility, and fundraising ability. Those are separate dimensions from infrastructure quality.

The practical implication for investors: tech due diligence cannot be shortcut by checking who else invested. A company backed by a16z at a $350M valuation may have a SOC 2 report filled with template placeholders, while a bootstrapped company in Toronto has multi-region GCP infrastructure with comprehensive monitoring. The only way to know is to look at the actual technical evidence.

The Investment Readiness Funnel

Starting from 485 companies, we applied progressive quality filters. The result is sobering.

FilterRemainingDrop-off
Total Portfolio485—
Type 2 (Operational evidence)485-0
Score 5+ (Minimum maturity)109-376
Score 7+ (Investment ready)19-90
Multi-Region + WAF77
Full Maturity204.1% of total

Only 20 companies (4.1%) meet the full maturity bar: SOC 2 Type 2 + multi-region + WAF + pen testing + daily backups + quarterly access reviews. The other 96% have concrete improvement opportunities — which is exactly where value creation lives.

For PE firms, this funnel is a value creation roadmap. The cheapest interventions are at the wide end: adding WAF to an API-first product takes a day via Cloudflare (cost: $20/month). Establishing quarterly access reviews is a process change that costs nothing. Moving from daily to real-time backups is a configuration change. These micro-interventions, applied across a portfolio of 20 companies, materially reduce portfolio-level risk at minimal cost.

The expensive interventions are at the narrow end: multi-region migration requires weeks of engineering work, cross-region data replication, and ongoing operational complexity. But for a company preparing for Series B or enterprise sales, the ability to say "we have multi-region failover with tested RTO/RPO" is a concrete competitive advantage. It's the difference between a SOC 2 report that says "we have a BCDR policy" and one that says "we tested failover to us-east-1 last quarter and recovered in 4 minutes."

Case Studies: From Data to Decisions

Numbers tell a story, but examples bring it to life. Here are three company profiles from our dataset that illustrate the range of tech maturity we observed — and what each profile means for an investor evaluating the opportunity.

Profile A: The Gold Standard (Score 7-8/10)

AI infrastructure company · AWS multi-region · 11 named vendors

8/10

This company's SOC 2 report reads like a technical architecture document. The network diagram names every service: ECS Fargate clusters, Aurora Serverless v2 with auto-scaling ACUs, ElastiCache Redis with TLS, Lambda functions in VPC with specific IAM roles, CloudFront CDN with WAF rules, and Route 53 for DNS. The system description explains not just what the product does but how it's built — down to the CDK v2 TypeScript infrastructure-as-code.

The vendor list is comprehensive: AWS, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Stripe, PostHog, Sentry, GitHub Actions, Clerk for auth. The DR section doesn't just say "we have a BCDR policy" — it describes tested failover procedures with specific recovery metrics.

Investor signal: This team knows their stack deeply and isn't afraid to document it. The transparency itself is the differentiator. This is a company where the engineering culture extends beyond code to compliance.

Profile B: The Template-Compliant Startup (Score 5-6/10)

AI SaaS company · AWS single-region · 2 named vendors

5/10

This company's report is textbook template compliance. The controls section reads identically to dozens of other reports in our dataset — the same sentences, the same structure, the same Accorp Partners boilerplate. The network diagram is a sample placeholder that was never replaced with the actual architecture. The signing authority line reads "Your Name Here." The system description says "headquartered at [Company Address]."

And yet: the company passed the audit with zero exceptions. All controls are "suitably designed." This is the paradox of template-driven compliance — you can have a clean SOC 2 opinion while revealing almost nothing about your actual technology.

Investor signal: This company got SOC 2 to check a box for enterprise sales. It doesn't mean the technology is bad — it means you need to look beyond the report. Request an architecture walkthrough, ask to see the monitoring dashboard, and evaluate the engineering team's depth directly.

Profile C: The Concerning Signal (Score 3-4/10)

Fintech-adjacent product · Single VM on basic cloud · 0 named vendors

3/10

This report raises genuine concerns. A single EC2 instance serves as the entire production environment for a payment-adjacent platform. No WAF, no CDN, no multi-AZ. The backup strategy is unspecified. The architecture diagram shows a single server with a database — the entire system is a potential single point of failure. Privacy and Processing Integrity trust criteria are excluded despite handling sensitive financial data.

The vendor list names only AWS — no monitoring tool, no CI/CD platform, no identity provider. Either the company runs everything in-house (unlikely for a small team) or they chose not to disclose their dependencies. Both interpretations are concerning.

Investor signal: The infrastructure doesn't match the ambition. If this company is raising a Series A for enterprise sales, the technology needs significant investment before it's production-ready for demanding customers. This is a clear "proceed with caution" signal that warrants a deep technical review before committing capital.

These three profiles represent the spectrum we see across the portfolio. The distribution is roughly 15% Profile A (strong foundations), 70% Profile B (template-compliant but opaque), and 15% Profile C (material concerns). Knowing which profile a company fits before the partner meeting saves weeks of back-and-forth and prevents surprises during diligence.

What Top Performers Do Differently

Comparing the top 20 companies (by score) against the bottom 20 reveals clear patterns:

The top performers share specific characteristics:

  • ✓ Multi-region deployment with active failover
  • ✓ WAF deployed on all internet-facing services
  • ✓ Named monitoring and SIEM tools (not just "monitoring tool")
  • ✓ 5+ vendors disclosed transparently in the compliance report
  • ✓ Branch protection with mandatory code review
  • ✓ Annual external penetration testing with remediation tracking
  • ✓ Quarterly access reviews for all accounts including admin

What This Means for Investors

The implications of this analysis differ by investor type, but the underlying message is the same: tech due diligence can be quantified, benchmarked, and systematized. The era of "I'll have my CTO read the SOC 2 on a flight" is over. With structured data on 485+ companies, we can now say with precision what "good" looks like — and price the risk of falling short.

For Growth Equity & Late-Stage

At growth stage, SOC 2 compliance is table stakes — the question is what's behind it. Companies preparing for IPO or strategic acquisition need to demonstrate operational excellence, not just policy existence. Focus on Tier 3 features: multi-region, stated RTO/RPO, cyber insurance, and quarterly access reviews. These are the metrics that acquirers and public market investors will scrutinize. A company that's still on a single region with no DR test results at $50M+ ARR has a material operational risk that should be reflected in valuation.

For PE Firms

The data reveals concrete value creation levers. Adding WAF across a portfolio is a one-day implementation per company via Cloudflare or AWS WAF. Establishing quarterly access reviews is a process change with zero cost. Migrating to multi-region takes weeks but dramatically reduces portfolio-level outage risk. These aren't hypothetical improvements — they're measurable moves that shift companies from Tier 2 to Tier 3 on the security ladder and improve their position for future fundraising or exit.

For VCs

Most SOC 2 reports are noise. The signal is in three places: the network architecture diagram (pages 18-19), the system description section (pages 14-17), and the vendor list. Everything else is template. Companies that name 5+ vendors, have detailed architecture diagrams, and exclude fewer trust criteria are the ones with genuine engineering culture — not just compliance-as-a-service.

10 Questions to Ask During Diligence

  1. What's your actual deployment pipeline? (CI/CD tool, deployment frequency)
  2. Show me your monitoring dashboard. (Proves operational maturity)
  3. What happens if your primary region goes down? (Multi-region / RTO/RPO)
  4. Who's your SIEM vendor? (If none, that's a signal)
  5. When was your last DR test, and what did you fix afterward?
  6. Why did you exclude Processing Integrity from SOC 2? (Tests AI governance awareness)
  7. What's your full vendor stack? Name every SaaS dependency.
  8. Show me your architecture diagram — the real one, not the SOC 2 version.
  9. How many engineers are on the team, and who's your on-call rotation?
  10. What would break if OpenAI raised prices 10x tomorrow?

What SOC 2 Reports Don't Tell You

This analysis is powerful, but it has limits. SOC 2 reports are compliance documents, not technical audits. They tell you whether controls exist, not whether they're effective in practice. There are critical dimensions of tech due diligence that no compliance report can answer:

Code Quality

Test coverage, technical debt, code review depth, dependency freshness. SOC 2 confirms branch protection exists but says nothing about what gets reviewed.

Performance & Scalability

Actual uptime, P99 latency, load test results, database query performance. These are operational metrics that live in monitoring dashboards, not audit reports.

Team Velocity

Deployment frequency, lead time for changes, DORA metrics. SOC 2 confirms a CI/CD pipeline exists but not how fast or reliably it ships.

AI Model Governance

Model evaluation frameworks, bias testing, prompt injection defenses, data governance. Most SOC 2 reports exclude Processing Integrity entirely.

The best tech due diligence combines automated compliance analysis (what we've done here) with targeted technical interviews, live architecture reviews, and operational metric audits. The SOC 2 analysis gives you the map — but you still need to walk the territory.
